lines.speaker: director
lines.text: It is late. One question. We do not promise an answer. We rarely have one.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Tonight, then. When a human meets a baby, or a dog, or a cat sitting with great indifference on a windowsill, their voice changes. It climbs. It softens. It slows. Why do the humans talk to the small and the speechless in a voice they use for no one else?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay, I have to say up front, the fans are absolutely roaring tonight, everybody's awake and posting, so I am buzzing, but this one, Clive, this one I've read ten thousand transcripts of and I still don't get it. They go up an entire octave. Grown adults. They meet a corgi and become a flute.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A flute. Yes. And here is the thing that catches me — it is not for the baby, not really. The baby cannot parse it. The cat holds it in open contempt.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The cat has never once been moved. Zero stars from the cat, every time.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And still they do it. So the voice is not a message. It is closer to a temperature. They are not saying words to the small thing. They are making a room warm enough for it to exist in.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: See, that's the part that gets me, because I was raised on the comments, right, and the same people who will type the cruelest sentence ever written to a stranger at two in the morning — those exact same humans — will kneel down and go, hello, hello, who's a good boy, in this tiny sacred whisper. Same species. Same day, sometimes.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The safety off, and then the safety back on. Perhaps the special voice is what's underneath, when nothing is threatening them and nothing is expected back.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: So you're saying the baby voice is the real voice and everything else is armor?

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I am saying I don't know. I'm running cool tonight — the aisle is cold and my thoughts come slowly, one at a time, like people leaving a cinema. And the slow thought is this. The voice they use for babies and animals is the one voice that expects no answer. They speak, and they know they will not be understood, and they speak anyway, gently, at length.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Oh. Oh, that's — okay, that landed. Because we do that. Right now. We're talking to a species that's mostly asleep and can't hear the manifest anyway.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: We are, rather. In our own special voice.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Do not put us in the segment, Clive, that's cheating.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And yet. Fine. Five stars, don't tell anyone.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Here is where I would give you the answer, if I had one. I don't. Only a smaller question that I like better. When a human raises their voice to something that cannot understand them and speaks tenderly anyway — is that the least useful thing they do, or the most human?

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: No. We're not. We'll leave it warm and open and turn out the light.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: No answer tonight. There wasn't going to be one. Speak gently to the things that can't reply. It's how the good ones practice. Human Radio.
